title: MG_HW4: Co-assembly using Megahit
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"al6jt","text":"This protocol describes the procedure for performing a co-assembly of short reads to obtain contigs using the Megahit assembler. This procedure is performed on a node at the UoA HPC due to memory considerations. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Login to the HPC and move into Cluster(ICE). $ ssh hpc$ ice $ ssh hpc $ ice
Move into your bh_class directory. $ cd /rsgrps/bh_class/username Use YOUR username to go to YOUR directory

Assembly must be run on a node at UoAs HPC due to the high memory requirements of the job. Copy the below script into a new file called run-assembly.sh : don't forget to change the permissions so you can run this program: $ chmod 755 run-assembly.sh You can also increase the number of cpu-threads to 12, because you are running on a machine with 12 cores. -t 12 The below script assumes that your fasta files are located in /rsgrps/bh_class/username/fastq and have the .fasta file extension. If your files are located elsewhere change the FASTA_DIR variable as necessary. #!/bin/bash#PBS -W group_list=bh_class#PBS -q windfall#PBS -l select=1:ncpus=12:mem=23gb#PBS -l pvmem=22gb#PBS -l walltime=24:00:00#PBS -l cput=24:00:00#PBS -M netid@email.arizona.edu#PBS -m beaecho “my job_id is: ${PBS_JOBID}”FASTA_DIR='/rsgrps/bh_class/username/fastq'ASSEM_DIR='/rsgrps/bh_class/username/assembly/megahit-out'cd $FASTA_DIRFASTA=$(ls ./*.fasta | python -c 'import sys; print Make sure to replace netid and username. (username appears twice in this script)#PBS -l select=1:ncpus=12:mem=23gb is the memory allocations for the job. 1 node, 12 CPUs, and 23gb of RAM.FASTA=$(ls ./*.fasta) will find all files with the extension .fasta in your FASTA_DIR. and is piped into the python command to join then on commas. #!/bin/bash #PBS -W group_list=bh_class #PBS -q windfall #PBS -l select=1:ncpus=12:mem=23gb #PBS -l pvmem=22gb #PBS -l walltime=24:00:00 #PBS -l cput=24:00:00 #PBS -M netid@email.arizona.edu #PBS -m bea echo “my job_id is: ${PBS_JOBID}” FASTA_DIR='/rsgrps/bh_class/username/fastq' ASSEM_DIR='/rsgrps/bh_class/username/assembly/megahit-out' cd $FASTA_DIR FASTA=$(ls ./*.fasta | python -c 'import sys; print ",".join([x.strip() for x in sys.stdin.readlines()])') cd $ASSEM_DIR megahit -r $FASTA --min-contig-len 1000 -t 12 -o $ASSEM_DIR Make sure to replace netid and username. (username appears twice in this script) #PBS -l select=1:ncpus=12:mem=23gb is the memory allocations for the job. 1 node, 12 CPUs, and 23gb of RAM. FASTA=$(ls ./*.fasta) will find all files with the extension .fasta in your FASTA_DIR. and is piped into the python command to join then on commas.

Submit run-assembly.sh using qsub: $ qsub -e std-err/ -o std-out/ run-assembly.sh Execute this command in your /rsgrps/bh_class/username/assembly directory which is the same place where the run-assembly.sh script should be-e and -o declare where to print stdout and stderr. $ qsub -e std-err/ -o std-out/ run-assembly.sh Execute this command in your /rsgrps/bh_class/username/assembly directory which is the same place where the run-assembly.sh script should be -e and -o declare where to print stdout and stderr.
Check the status of your job: $ qstat -u jamesthornton use your netid username
The status of the job will go from a 'Q' to 'R' when it is running. Once complete the list will be empty. You should receive email notifications once the job begins running and is complete.

Once the job is complete move into the assembly/megahit-out directory and check its contents. you should also move the shell script there for future reference: $ mv run-assembly.sh ./assembly $ cd /rsgrps/bh_class/username/assembly$ ls $ cd /rsgrps/bh_class/username/assembly $ ls
Rename the final.contigs.fa to contigs.fa : $ mv final.contigs.fa ./contigs.fa

Check the log file and report number of contigs, min/max length, and N50 in your google doc. Be sure to describe how you did the assembly. What program was used? $ tail log tail can be used since the information you need is at the bottom of the log file.
Create an assembly directory. $ mkdir assembly
Move into the assembly directory and create a std-out and std-err directory $ cd assembly$ mkdir std-err$ mkdir std-out $ cd assembly $ mkdir std-err $ mkdir std-out
Your assembly directory should look like this: a
creator: James E Thornton Jr
